Sciences.title: Unit 1 – Energy in the Biosphere (Criterion A Practice)

questions.q: Which of the following BEST describes an ecosystem?
options: A group of animals living together in the same place
A system where living organisms interact only with each other
A community of living organisms interacting with each other and with their non-living environment
A collection of only animals that do not rely on their surroundings
questions.answer: A community of living organisms interacting with each other and with their non-living environment
questions.hint: An ecosystem includes BOTH biotic (living) AND abiotic (non-living) factors working together.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of an ABIOTIC factor in an ecosystem?
questions.answer: Sunlight
questions.hint: Abiotic means NON-living. Think about which option is not a living organism.

questions.q: During photosynthesis, what raw materials do plants use?
questions.answer: Sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide
questions.hint: Remember the equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

questions.q: Which trophic level is responsible for entering energy from the Sun into the ecosystem?
questions.answer: Producers
questions.hint: Which organisms can make their own food using sunlight through photosynthesis?

questions.q: A grasshopper eats grass. A frog eats the grasshopper. What trophic level does the FROG occupy?
questions.answer: Secondary Consumer (Trophic Level 3)
questions.hint: The frog eats an animal (grasshopper) that eats plants. Count the levels: grass → grasshopper → frog.

questions.q: In a trophic pyramid of energy, approximately what percentage of energy is transferred from one level to the next?
questions.hint: Most energy is lost through respiration, heat, movement, and waste at each level.

questions.q: Outline the difference between biotic and abiotic factors in an ecosystem. Give TWO examples of each and explain how they interact with each other.
questions.answer: Biotic factors are living components of an ecosystem, such as plants, animals, fungi, and bacteria. Abiotic factors are non-living components, such as sunlight, water, temperature, and soil. They interact because living organisms depend on non-living factors to survive: for example, plants (biotic) need sunlight and water (abiotic) to perform photosynthesis, and animals (biotic) need water (abiotic) to drink and air (abiotic) to breathe. If abiotic factors change (e.g. less water), biotic factors are affected (plants die, animals lose food).
questions.hint: Think about what 'living' vs 'non-living' means, and how they depend on each other.

questions.q: Outline the importance of producers (photosynthetic organisms) in an ecosystem. Include the photosynthesis equation and explain their role in the food chain.
questions.answer: Producers (plants, algae, some bacteria) are essential because they convert solar energy into chemical energy (glucose) through photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. They form the BASE of all food chains and food webs, providing food and energy for all consumers. Without producers, no energy from the Sun could enter the ecosystem. They also release oxygen needed for respiration and remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, helping maintain environmental balance and supporting biodiversity.
questions.hint: Think about where ALL energy in a food chain originally comes from, and what would happen without plants.

questions.q: Deforestation is destroying bird habitats. Suggest what would happen to the food chain if the bird population decreases significantly. Then suggest TWO solutions to protect birds.
questions.answer: If bird populations decrease: (1) Insects that birds eat would increase in number (population explosion), potentially becoming pests. (2) Predators that eat birds (like snakes or hawks) would lose a food source and their populations would also decline. (3) Plants that depend on birds for seed dispersal or pollination would be affected. The entire food web would become unbalanced. Solutions: (1) Create protected natural reserves where deforestation is prohibited. (2) Plant new trees and restore habitats to give birds places to nest.
questions.hint: Think step by step: what eats birds? What do birds eat? What happens when one link in the chain is removed?

questions.q: Interpret the following biomass pyramid data: Producers = 10,000 g/m², Primary Consumers = 1,000 g/m², Secondary Consumers = 100 g/m², Tertiary Consumers = 10 g/m². Describe the general trend, include the data to support it, and explain WHY producers are the most important level.
questions.answer: This is an upright biomass pyramid (terrestrial). The general trend shows that biomass DECREASES as you move up each trophic level. Producers have the most biomass at 10,000 g/m², which is 10 times more than primary consumers (1,000 g/m²), 100 times more than secondary consumers (100 g/m²), and 1,000 times more than tertiary consumers (10 g/m²). Only about 10% of biomass is transferred to the next level because energy is lost through respiration, heat, movement, incomplete digestion, and excretion. Producers are the most important level because they are the foundation — they capture solar energy through photosynthesis and make it available to the entire ecosystem. Without them, no other trophic level could exist.
questions.hint: Compare the numbers between each level. What is the ratio? Why does this happen? Think about energy loss.

Sciences.title: Unit 1 – Energy in the Biosphere (Practice Test 2)

questions.q: Which of the following is a TERRESTRIAL ecosystem?
questions.answer: A desert
questions.hint: Terrestrial means 'land-based'. Which of these ecosystems is found on land, not in water?

questions.q: What are the TWO main products of photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Glucose and oxygen
questions.hint: Look at the RIGHT side of the photosynthesis equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

questions.q: Fungi and bacteria that break down dead organisms and return nutrients to the soil are called:
questions.answer: Decomposers
questions.hint: Which type of organism recycles nutrients from dead matter back into the ecosystem?

questions.q: What is the correct ORDER of organization from smallest to largest in biology?
questions.answer: Organism → Population → Community → Ecosystem → Biosphere
questions.hint: Start with a single living thing, then a group of the same species, then different species together...

questions.q: An eagle eats a snake that eats a frog that eats insects that eat grass. What is the eagle in this food chain?
questions.answer: Quaternary consumer (apex predator)
questions.hint: Count the levels: grass(1) → insect(2) → frog(3) → snake(4) → eagle(?). The eagle is at the top.

questions.q: What is the main difference between a food CHAIN and a food WEB?
options: A food chain only includes producers, a food web includes consumers
A food chain shows a single path of energy flow, a food web shows multiple interconnected paths
A food web only exists in aquatic ecosystems
A food chain is more accurate than a food web
questions.answer: A food chain shows a single path of energy flow, a food web shows multiple interconnected paths
questions.hint: Think about the word 'web' — what does a spider's web look like compared to a single chain?

questions.q: Outline why photosynthetic organisms are considered autotrophs. Explain the difference between autotrophs and heterotrophs, giving TWO examples of each.
questions.answer: Photosynthetic organisms are called autotrophs because they can produce their own food (chemical energy) using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide through photosynthesis. 'Auto' means self and 'troph' means food — so autotrophs are self-feeders. Examples of autotrophs: plants and algae. Heterotrophs, on the other hand, cannot make their own food and must consume other organisms to obtain energy. 'Hetero' means other — so heterotrophs feed on others. Examples of heterotrophs: rabbits (herbivores that eat plants) and lions (carnivores that eat other animals). The key difference is that autotrophs produce energy from sunlight, while heterotrophs must eat other organisms.
questions.hint: Break down the words: 'auto' = self, 'hetero' = other, 'troph' = food/nutrition.

questions.q: A food chain in a forest ecosystem is: Oak tree → Caterpillar → Robin → Hawk. State the trophic level of EACH organism and outline what would happen to the ecosystem if a disease killed most of the caterpillars.
questions.answer: Oak tree = Producer (Trophic Level 1, autotroph). Caterpillar = Primary Consumer (Trophic Level 2, herbivore). Robin = Secondary Consumer (Trophic Level 3, carnivore/insectivore). Hawk = Tertiary Consumer (Trophic Level 4, apex predator). If a disease killed most caterpillars: (1) Oak trees would grow more because fewer caterpillars are eating their leaves. (2) Robins would lose their main food source, so their population would decrease or they would need to find alternative food. (3) Hawks that eat robins would also be affected because fewer robins means less food for them. (4) The entire food chain would become unbalanced, showing how removing one link affects all others.
questions.hint: Label each organism first, then think step by step: what happens ABOVE and BELOW the caterpillar in the chain?

questions.q: Explain why energy DECREASES as you move up the trophic pyramid. If producers capture 10,000 kJ of energy, calculate how much energy reaches the tertiary consumers. Use the 10% rule.
questions.answer: Energy decreases at each trophic level because organisms use most of the energy they consume for their own life processes: cellular respiration (to move, grow, and stay warm), incomplete digestion (not all food is digested), and excretion (waste products). Only about 10% of the energy at each level is passed to the next. Calculation using the 10% rule: Producers = 10,000 kJ → Primary Consumers = 10,000 × 10% = 1,000 kJ → Secondary Consumers = 1,000 × 10% = 100 kJ → Tertiary Consumers = 100 × 10% = 10 kJ. So tertiary consumers receive only 10 kJ out of the original 10,000 kJ — that is only 0.1% of the original energy! This explains why food chains rarely have more than 4-5 levels: there simply isn't enough energy left to support more levels.
questions.hint: At each level, multiply by 0.10 (10%). Do this three times starting from 10,000 kJ.

questions.q: Scientists discover that a lake ecosystem is losing its algae population due to pollution. Interpret how this would affect the entire aquatic food web. Include effects on at least THREE different trophic levels and suggest TWO actions humans could take to help restore the ecosystem.
questions.answer: Algae are the main producers in aquatic ecosystems. If algae disappear due to pollution: (1) PRODUCERS level: Less photosynthesis means less oxygen in the water and less chemical energy entering the food web. (2) PRIMARY CONSUMERS: Zooplankton and small fish that eat algae would lose their food source, causing their populations to decline. (3) SECONDARY CONSUMERS: Larger fish that eat zooplankton would also decrease because there is less food available. (4) TERTIARY CONSUMERS: Top predators like eagles or large fish would be affected as the entire food web collapses from the bottom up. Additionally, less photosynthesis means less oxygen dissolved in water, which could cause fish to suffocate. Actions to restore the ecosystem: (1) Reduce pollution by banning or limiting chemical dumping and pesticide runoff into the lake. (2) Create water treatment systems to filter pollutants before water enters the lake. These actions would allow algae populations to recover and rebuild the food web from the base up.
questions.hint: Start from the bottom (algae = producers) and work your way UP the food web. Think about oxygen too!

Sciences.title: Unit 1 – Energy in the Biosphere (Practice Test 3)

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a RENEWABLE resource?
questions.answer: Wind energy
questions.hint: Renewable resources can be replenished naturally. Which one regenerates continuously?

questions.q: In an aquatic ecosystem, which organisms form the PRIMARY SOURCE of food for all other living things?
questions.answer: Algae and aquatic plants
questions.hint: What photosynthesize in water to produce glucose and oxygen?

questions.q: A polar bear eats a seal that eats fish that eat zooplankton that eat algae. Which trophic level do zooplankton occupy?
questions.answer: Primary Consumer
questions.hint: Zooplankton eat algae (producers), so they are the first consumers in the chain.

questions.q: What is the PRIMARY reason that LESS biomass exists at higher trophic levels?
options: Consumers at higher levels are larger animals
Energy is lost at each level through respiration, heat, and waste
Predators hunt more efficiently at higher levels
questions.answer: Energy is lost at each level through respiration, heat, and waste
questions.hint: Think about where energy is lost in the trophic pyramid.

questions.q: Which statement BEST explains how biotic and abiotic factors work together in an ecosystem?
options: Biotic factors only depend on other biotic factors
Abiotic factors provide the conditions for biotic factors to survive and thrive
Biotic factors create the non-living environment
They function completely independently of each other
questions.answer: Abiotic factors provide the conditions for biotic factors to survive and thrive
questions.hint: Living things need non-living resources to survive.

questions.q: When a plant dies and decomposes, what happens to the energy stored in its body?
options: It is returned to the soil as nutrients that can be used by new plants
It transforms into new fossil fuels
It is stored permanently in the ecosystem
questions.answer: It is returned to the soil as nutrients that can be used by new plants
questions.hint: What role do decomposers play in nutrient cycling?

questions.q: Outline what happens when a PRIMARY CONSUMER herbivore (such as a deer) is removed from a forest ecosystem. Include effects on producers and secondary consumers in your answer.
questions.answer: If primary consumer herbivores like deer are removed: (1) PRODUCERS (plants and trees): Without deer grazing, plants would grow unchecked and vegetation would become overgrown and dense. This could lead to competition among plants for sunlight and nutrients. (2) SECONDARY CONSUMERS (predators like wolves or mountain lions): These predators would lose a major food source, causing their populations to decline significantly or starve. They would need to find alternative prey or migrate elsewhere. (3) OVERALL ECOSYSTEM: The food chain becomes disrupted. Without predators controlling the vegetation, the forest ecosystem becomes unbalanced. This shows how removing one link in the food chain affects the entire ecosystem structure.
questions.hint: Think about what the deer eats (plants) and what eats the deer (predators). Both are affected.

questions.q: Suggest why tropical rainforest ecosystems support MORE biodiversity than desert ecosystems. Consider both abiotic and biotic factors in your answer.
questions.answer: Tropical rainforests support more biodiversity than deserts because: ABIOTIC FACTORS: (1) Rainforests have abundant sunlight year-round, allowing continuous photosynthesis and energy production. (2) High rainfall provides plenty of water for all organisms. (3) Warm, stable temperatures support diverse life. (4) Rich, nutrient-dense soil from decomposition supports many plant species. In contrast, deserts have: limited water (arid), extreme temperature fluctuations, and poor soil. BIOTIC FACTORS: (1) The diverse plant life in rainforests provides many different food sources and habitats for animals. (2) Multiple producers support multiple consumers at each trophic level. (3) Complex food webs mean organisms have alternative food sources if one species declines. (4) High competition drives specialization and biodiversity. Deserts support fewer species because there is less energy available, fewer plant types, and simpler food chains.
questions.hint: Rainforests have favorable abiotic conditions that allow more producers to grow. More producers = more food = more consumers.

questions.q: A farmer uses heavy pesticides on crops, killing most of the insects in the area. Outline the SHORT-TERM effects on the food web and SUGGEST how this might create a problem EVEN FOR HUMANS in the long term.
questions.answer: SHORT-TERM EFFECTS: (1) Insects (primary consumers) are killed by pesticides. (2) Birds that eat insects lose their food source and populations decline. (3) Crops initially have fewer insects eating them, so farmers see bigger harvests temporarily. LONG-TERM PROBLEMS FOR HUMANS: (1) Without insects like bees and butterflies, POLLINATION declines, meaning crops cannot reproduce and produce seeds or fruits. This eventually reduces crop yields despite fewer insects eating them. (2) Insects are crucial decomposers; without them, dead organic matter accumulates and nutrients are not recycled back into soil, reducing soil fertility. (3) Without birds (which ate insects), pest insects that resist pesticides multiply unchecked. (4) Pesticides accumulate in the soil and water, affecting human health and drinking water quality. This demonstrates that eliminating one trophic level (insects) disrupts the entire ecosystem and eventually harms humans who depend on that ecosystem.
questions.hint: Bees and insects do more than just eat — they pollinate. What happens if there are no pollinators?

questions.q: A mangrove forest ecosystem is being destroyed by coastal development. Interpret how the loss of mangroves would affect the ocean food web. Consider energy flow, biodiversity, and at least THREE trophic levels in your explanation. What can be done to protect remaining mangrove ecosystems?
questions.answer: Mangroves are crucial PRIMARY PRODUCERS in coastal ecosystems. EFFECTS OF THEIR LOSS: (1) PRODUCERS LEVEL: Mangrove vegetation disappears, reducing oxygen-producing photosynthesis. The water becomes murky and oxygen-poor. (2) PRIMARY CONSUMERS: Fish larvae, crustaceans (shrimp, crabs), and zooplankton lose their nursery habitat and food source. Their populations collapse by 80-90%. (3) SECONDARY CONSUMERS: Larger fish that eat small fish and crustaceans lose their prey. Fish populations decline, affecting fishermen and local food security. (4) TERTIARY CONSUMERS: Marine birds (herons, eagles) and dolphins that depend on fish lose food and habitat. (5) ENERGY FLOW DISRUPTION: Mangroves export nutrients to the ocean. Without them, less energy enters the marine food web, reducing overall productivity. (6) BIODIVERSITY: Mangrove ecosystems support 75% of commercial fish species at some life stage. Loss reduces biodiversity from the microscopic level to apex predators. SOLUTIONS: (1) Establish marine protected areas where mangrove destruction is prohibited. (2) Restore degraded mangroves through replanting programs. (3) Create sustainable coastal development that works with mangrove ecosystems rather than destroying them.
questions.hint: Mangroves are found where land meets ocean. They are the 'nurseries' of the sea. What happens when the nursery closes?

Sciences.title: Unit 1 – Energy in the Biosphere (Practice Test 4)

questions.q: Which of the following statements CORRECTLY describes the relationship between photosynthesis and cellular respiration?
options: They are the same process occurring in different organisms
Photosynthesis produces glucose that is later used in cellular respiration for energy
Cellular respiration only occurs in animals, photosynthesis only in plants
They do not interact with each other in any way
questions.answer: Photosynthesis produces glucose that is later used in cellular respiration for energy
questions.hint: Photosynthesis makes food. Respiration uses that food for energy.

questions.q: In a marine ecosystem, diatoms are single-celled algae that form the base of most ocean food chains. What trophic level do diatoms occupy?
questions.answer: Producer
questions.hint: Diatoms photosynthesize. They make their own food.

questions.q: Which of the following is the BEST example of an inverted pyramid (where biomass increases at higher trophic levels)?
options: A forest with many trees and few herbivores
An aquatic ecosystem where one large whale eats many small fish that eat zooplankton
A grassland with abundant grass and few predators
An ecosystem with producers, primary consumers, and secondary consumers
questions.answer: An aquatic ecosystem where one large whale eats many small fish that eat zooplankton
questions.hint: Think about a whale being huge but eating tiny fish. What does the biomass look like?

questions.q: What is the MAIN reason that decomposers are essential to ecosystem health?
options: They produce energy like producers do
They consume more energy than predators
They break down dead matter and return nutrients to the soil for reuse
They prevent all organisms from dying
questions.answer: They break down dead matter and return nutrients to the soil for reuse
questions.hint: Decomposers are nature's recyclers.

questions.q: A farmer wants to increase crop yield by adding MORE chemical fertilizer each year. Based on ecosystem principles, what is the MAIN risk of this practice?
options: It will increase the number of insects in the area
Excess nutrients will run off into waterways, causing algal blooms and dead zones where aquatic life cannot survive
It will make the soil harder and less suitable for plants
It will attract more predators to the farm
questions.answer: Excess nutrients will run off into waterways, causing algal blooms and dead zones where aquatic life cannot survive
questions.hint: When too many nutrients enter water, algae multiply rapidly. What happens then?

questions.q: Which environmental factor would be MOST IMPORTANT in determining whether a particular area could support a large population of polar bears?
options: The availability of seals and other prey in nearby waters
The amount of plant material available for grazing
questions.answer: The availability of seals and other prey in nearby waters
questions.hint: What do polar bears eat?

questions.q: Outline the concept of ENERGY EFFICIENCY in a food chain. Why is it inefficient to raise cattle on grain farms instead of eating the grain directly? Use the 10% rule in your explanation.
questions.answer: Energy efficiency refers to how much energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next. Only about 10% of energy is passed from one level to the next; 90% is lost to respiration, heat, and waste. GRAIN AS DIRECT FOOD (Direct): 100% of grain energy goes to humans. GRAIN → CATTLE → HUMANS (Indirect): Humans eat grain (100 energy units) → Cattle convert it (only 10 energy units stored as meat) → Humans eat cattle (only 1 energy unit reaches humans). So for every 100 units of grain energy, only 1 unit reaches humans as beef. This means we waste 99 units of potential human food by feeding grain to cattle and then eating the cattle. It is FAR MORE EFFICIENT for humans to eat the grain directly and get 100 energy units rather than eating cattle meat and getting only 1 energy unit. This is why vegetarian diets can feed more people per acre of farmland than meat-based diets.
questions.hint: Grain → cattle = 10% → humans = 10% of 10%. How much energy is left at the end?

questions.q: A lake is experiencing harmful algal blooms (excessive algae growth) due to agricultural runoff containing nitrogen and phosphorus. Suggest what is happening to the ecosystem at each trophic level and propose THREE solutions to stop the algal blooms.
questions.answer: TROPHIC LEVEL EFFECTS: (1) PRODUCERS (algae): Excess nutrients cause algae to multiply explosively. The lake becomes thick with algae. (2) Oxygen levels: As algae die and decompose, decomposers use up oxygen in the water, creating oxygen-poor 'dead zones.' (3) PRIMARY CONSUMERS: Fish and zooplankton suffocate from lack of oxygen. Their populations crash. (4) SECONDARY & TERTIARY CONSUMERS: Birds and larger fish that depend on smaller fish lose food sources and starve. (5) OVERALL: The food web collapses; biodiversity plummets. SOLUTIONS: (1) Reduce agricultural runoff by requiring farmers to use buffer zones (vegetated areas) between fields and waterways. Plants absorb excess nutrients before water enters the lake. (2) Ban or restrict the use of phosphate fertilizers and promote organic farming methods that release fewer nutrients. (3) Install water treatment systems that filter nitrogen and phosphorus from runoff before it reaches the lake. Additionally, (4) Clean up existing algal blooms mechanically to restore oxygen levels while long-term solutions take effect.
questions.hint: The problem is TOO MUCH nutrient entering the ecosystem. How do you stop nutrients from reaching the lake?

questions.q: A coral reef ecosystem is being threatened by rising ocean temperatures and ocean acidification (caused by increased CO₂ in the atmosphere). Interpret how these abiotic changes would affect the coral reef food web. Include at least THREE trophic levels and suggest what humans can do to prevent further damage.
questions.answer: ABIOTIC CHANGES: Rising temperature and acidification are stressors. EFFECTS ON FOOD WEB: (1) PRODUCERS: Corals are symbiotic organisms that rely on algae living inside them. Rising temperature causes coral bleaching (algae die and corals turn white). Without algae, corals cannot produce energy and starve. Dead corals no longer photosynthesize or provide habitat. (2) PRIMARY CONSUMERS: Fish, sea turtles, and invertebrates that eat coral or live in coral habitat lose their homes and food source. Their populations decline drastically. (3) SECONDARY CONSUMERS: Sharks and larger fish that hunt smaller reef fish lose prey and food security. (4) OVERALL BIODIVERSITY: Coral reefs support 25% of all marine fish species despite covering only 1% of the ocean floor. Their collapse devastates marine biodiversity. Ocean acidification dissolves shells of mollusks and crustaceans, making it harder for them to survive. SOLUTIONS: (1) Reduce greenhouse gas emissions by using renewable energy instead of fossil fuels. This slows global warming and reduces CO₂ entering the atmosphere. (2) Establish marine protected areas where fishing is restricted, allowing fish populations to recover and become more resilient to climate stress. (3) Restore and transplant healthy corals to degraded reefs. (4) Reduce local stressors like pollution and overfishing so reefs can better withstand climate change impacts.
questions.hint: Temperature stress kills the algae inside corals. What happens to the coral when the algae die? What eats coral?

questions.q: Interpret the following scenario: A invasive species of snake is introduced to an island where it had no natural predators. Within 5 years, the snake population explodes and the populations of small mammals and birds on the island decrease by 90%. What has happened to the island's food web structure and biodiversity? Suggest what should be done to restore the ecosystem.
questions.answer: WHAT HAPPENED: (1) INVASIVE PREDATOR (snake): With no natural predators, the snake population grows exponentially, following an 'S-curve' explosion. The snake becomes a new tertiary/apex consumer. (2) PRIMARY & SECONDARY CONSUMERS: Small mammals (rodents) and birds that were the secondary/tertiary consumers are now prey for the snakes. Their populations crash by 90% due to predation pressure. (3) FOOD WEB DISRUPTION: The island food web becomes unbalanced. Fewer birds means fewer predators of insects, so insects multiply. Fewer mammals means less grazing/seed dispersal, affecting plants. The ecosystem structure collapses. (4) BIODIVERSITY LOSS: Species that depended on birds or mammals disappear. Herbivores that relied on bird-dispersed seeds decline. The island loses its native food chains and many species go extinct. PRIMARY CONSEQUENCES: Simple food webs (with fewer species) are fragile and collapse easily. The island ecosystem becomes dominated by just snakes and insects, losing the complexity and resilience it had before. RESTORATION SOLUTIONS: (1) IMMEDIATE: Manually remove snakes (hunting, trapping) to reduce their population to manageable levels. (2) MEDIUM-TERM: Reintroduce native mammal and bird species from other populations. (3) LONG-TERM: Establish strict biosecurity to prevent future invasive species introductions. (4) PROTECT: Create protected areas where snakes cannot enter, preserving remaining native species. (5) MONITOR: Track food web recovery and adjust strategies as needed.
questions.hint: An invasive predator with no enemies grows without limit. What does this do to its prey? What cascades down the food web from there?

Sciences.title: Unit 1 – Energy in the Biosphere (Practice Test 5)

questions.q: Which of the following is the PRIMARY energy source that powers ALL ecosystems on Earth?
options: Chemical energy stored in fossil fuels
questions.answer: Sunlight and solar radiation
questions.hint: Where do producers get their energy for photosynthesis?

questions.q: In a savanna ecosystem, herbivores like zebras and wildebeest are primary consumers. If a severe drought kills most of the grass (producers), what would be the IMMEDIATE effect on zebra populations?
options: Zebra populations would increase due to less competition for grass
Zebra populations would decrease due to lack of food
Zebra would evolve to become carnivores
questions.answer: Zebra populations would decrease due to lack of food
questions.hint: Primary consumers depend directly on producers for food.

questions.q: Which statement correctly explains why nutrients MUST be recycled in ecosystems?
options: To prevent soil from becoming too rich with nutrients
To ensure that nutrients do not accumulate at the top of the food chain and instead become available to producers again
To reduce the number of decomposers needed
To increase energy transfer between trophic levels
questions.answer: To ensure that nutrients do not accumulate at the top of the food chain and instead become available to producers again
questions.hint: Decomposers break down dead matter and return what to the soil?

questions.q: An organism that can survive by eating both plants and meat (like a bear or a human) is called:
questions.answer: An omnivore
questions.hint: 'Omni' means all. What eats everything?

questions.q: Which of the following pairs shows organisms from the SAME trophic level?
questions.answer: Rabbit and wolf
questions.hint: Which two eat the same TYPE of food — both are herbivores or both are carnivores?

questions.q: If humans continue to over-harvest fish from the ocean, causing fish populations to collapse, which trophic level would be affected FIRST?
options: Secondary consumers (smaller fish that eat zooplankton)
questions.answer: Secondary consumers (smaller fish that eat zooplankton)
questions.hint: If we remove the larger fish that people eat, what does the food web look like?

questions.q: Outline how LOGGING in a rainforest affects the ecosystem's CARBON CYCLE and ENERGY FLOW. Explain the consequences for global climate.
questions.answer: LOGGING EFFECTS ON CARBON CYCLE: (1) REMOVES PRODUCERS: Massive trees that photosynthesize and store carbon in their wood are cut down. The rate of CO₂ absorption from the atmosphere decreases dramatically. (2) RELEASES STORED CARBON: When wood is burned or decomposes, the stored carbon is released back into the atmosphere as CO₂. (3) DESTROYS DECOMPOSER HABITAT: Without trees and vegetation, soil decomposers decline, reducing nutrient cycling and carbon storage in soil. (4) DISRUPTS FOOD CHAIN: The forest floor ecosystem that depends on leaf litter and dead wood collapses. LOGGING EFFECTS ON ENERGY FLOW: (1) REDUCES PRODUCERS: With fewer trees, less sunlight energy is captured through photosynthesis. Less energy enters the ecosystem. (2) FOOD WEB COLLAPSE: Animals that depended on the forest (birds, insects, mammals) lose habitat and food, populations crash. (3) TROPHIC PYRAMID INVERTS: Reduced producer biomass means fewer primary consumers can be supported, then fewer secondary consumers. CONSEQUENCES FOR GLOBAL CLIMATE: (1) INCREASED ATMOSPHERIC CO₂: Removing forests releases stored carbon and reduces CO₂ absorption, contributing to global warming. (2) POSITIVE FEEDBACK LOOP: Warmer temperatures cause more forest stress and death, releasing more CO₂. (3) ALTERED RAINFALL PATTERNS: Rainforests create their own climate by releasing moisture. Deforestation reduces rainfall globally, affecting agriculture. (4) ECOSYSTEM COLLAPSE: Loss of rainforest biodiversity means loss of potential medicines and genetic resources. The climate consequences are severe and long-lasting.
questions.hint: Trees are both energy producers AND carbon storage. What happens when you remove them?

questions.q: A student claims that 'humans are not part of the food web because we are intelligent and can choose not to eat living things.' Outline why this statement is INCORRECT and explain how humans FIT INTO natural food webs.
questions.answer: THE STATEMENT IS INCORRECT because: (1) HUMANS ARE ORGANISMS: Despite our intelligence, humans are animals that must eat to survive. We need energy to live, think, and move. (2) HUMANS ARE CONSUMERS: We are primary consumers when we eat plants (salads, grains, fruits) and secondary/tertiary consumers when we eat animals that eat plants or other animals. We are also omnivores — we eat both plants and meat. (3) HUMANS FIT INTO FOOD CHAINS: Examples of food chains humans are part of: • Plant → Human (primary consumer) • Plant → Cow → Human (secondary consumer) • Plant → Small fish → Salmon → Human (tertiary consumer) (4) INTELLIGENCE DOESN'T EXEMPT US: Although humans are intelligent and can CHOOSE what to eat, we still MUST EAT something from the food web to survive. We cannot photosynthesize. Intelligence allows us to farm, breed animals, and choose our diet, but does not place us outside the food web. (5) WE AFFECT ECOSYSTEMS: Because humans are consumers with enormous populations and global reach, we have MASSIVE impacts on food webs — through fishing, hunting, agriculture, and habitat destruction. We significantly affect energy flow in every ecosystem. CONCLUSION: Humans are definitely part of natural food webs as consumers. We depend on producers (plants) and other consumers (animals) for survival, just like any other organism on Earth.
questions.hint: Can humans make their own food from sunlight like plants do? What do we have to eat to survive?

questions.q: Compare and contrast a TEMPERATE FOREST ecosystem with a GRASSLAND/PRAIRIE ecosystem. Include differences in abiotic factors, biotic factors, food web structure, and energy flow. Which has higher biodiversity and why?
questions.answer: ABIOTIC FACTORS: TEMPERATE FOREST: (1) Receives 750-1500 mm rainfall annually. (2) Temperature ranges from cold winters (-5°C to 0°C) to warm summers (15°C-25°C). (3) Moderate sunlight (blocked by dense canopy in some areas). (4) Rich, deep soil from leaf litter decomposition. GRASSLAND/PRAIRIE: (1) Receives 250-750 mm rainfall (drier). (2) Temperature extremes: very hot summers (30°C+), cold winters (-10°C or below). (3) Full sunlight (no trees to block). (4) Shallow soil with dense root mat from grasses. BIOTIC FACTORS: TEMPERATE FOREST: (1) Tall trees (producers) dominate. Many herbivores (deer, rabbits). Predators (wolves, bears). Insects are abundant. GRASSLAND: (1) Grasses are main producers (no trees). Large herbivores (bison, zebras). Predators (lions, eagles). Fewer insect varieties than forests. FOOD WEB STRUCTURE: TEMPERATE FOREST: Complex, many-layered food webs. Multiple pathways. Example: Acorns → Squirrels, Deer, Birds → Hawks, Wolves, Bears. Producers include understory plants, shrubs, and trees. GRASSLAND: Simpler food webs with fewer layers. Example: Grasses → Grasshoppers, Rabbits, Bison → Predators (eagles, lions). ENERGY FLOW: TEMPERATE FOREST: High energy capture (many trees photosynthesize year-round, despite winter dormancy). Abundant decomposition of leaf litter. Efficient nutrient cycling. GRASSLAND: Lower initial energy capture (fewer plants overall). High sun exposure but drier conditions limit photosynthesis. Fire and grazing animals recycle nutrients. BIODIVERSITY: TEMPERATE FORESTS have HIGHER biodiversity because: (1) Diverse vegetation structure (trees, shrubs, herbs) creates varied habitats. (2) More food sources year-round support more species. (3) Complex vertical layers (canopy, understory, forest floor) support specialized organisms. (4) Abundant leaf litter supports rich decomposer communities. GRASSLANDS have lower biodiversity due to: (1) Simpler vegetation (mainly grasses). (2) Fewer food sources and habitats. (3) Harsher climate extremes limit species adapted to survive. (4) Food webs are simpler with fewer niches available. CONCLUSION: Temperate forests support more species because their complex structure, abundant energy flow, and favorable climate provide more opportunities for specialization and niche differentiation.
questions.hint: Which ecosystem has more types of plants and therefore more types of animals that depend on them?

questions.q: Interpret the effects of OCEAN PLASTIC POLLUTION on marine ecosystems. Discuss how plastic affects at least THREE trophic levels and the flow of energy through the food web. What can humans do to reduce this problem at individual, community, and global levels?
questions.answer: OCEAN PLASTIC EFFECTS ON TROPHIC LEVELS: (1) PRIMARY CONSUMERS (zooplankton, small fish): Mistake plastic microparticles for food (phytoplankton). Eat plastic, which blocks their digestive systems, preventing nutrient absorption. Many starve with full stomachs. Populations decline. (2) SECONDARY CONSUMERS (medium fish, seabirds): Eat small fish and zooplankton that contain plastic. Plastic accumulates in their bodies (bioaccumulation). May become entangled in plastic nets and bags, suffering injury or death. Population decline due to starvation and injury. (3) TERTIARY CONSUMERS (sharks, dolphins, sea lions): Eat smaller fish that have accumulated plastic. Plastic concentrates even further in their bodies through biomagnification — top predators have the highest plastic concentration. Suffer organ damage, reduced fertility, and population collapse. (4) ENERGY FLOW DISRUPTION: Plastic reduces energy transfer efficiency. Organisms waste energy trying to digest indigestible plastic. Less energy available for growth and reproduction. Entire food web becomes less productive. SOLUTIONS BY LEVEL: INDIVIDUAL ACTIONS: (1) Reduce plastic use — carry reusable bags, bottles, and containers. (2) Properly dispose of plastic waste so it doesn't reach oceans. (3) Participate in beach cleanups. (4) Choose sustainable products and support eco-friendly companies. COMMUNITY ACTIONS: (1) Advocate for local bans on single-use plastics (bags, straws, cutlery). (2) Organize education campaigns in schools about ocean plastic. (3) Push municipalities to improve waste management and recycling infrastructure. (4) Support marine protected areas and restoration projects. GLOBAL ACTIONS: (1) International agreements to ban problematic plastics globally. (2) Development of biodegradable alternatives to conventional plastics. (3) Investment in ocean cleanup technology (e.g., Boyan Slat's Ocean Cleanup Project). (4) Regulation of industrial plastic production and incentives for sustainable alternatives. (5) Support developing countries in building waste management systems. CONCLUSION: Ocean plastic pollution demonstrates how human activities disrupt entire food webs and energy flow in marine ecosystems. Solutions require action at all levels — individual choices, community commitment, and global policy change.
questions.hint: Plastic is not food, but animals eat it anyway. How does this affect the food chain from zooplankton all the way up to sharks?
